lesson.title: Maps
lesson.summary: Key-value pairs — look up values by a key.

sections.heading: What is a map
sections.bodyHtml:
A map stores pairs of keys and values. Given a key you get back its value — like a dictionary or phone book.

Declare a map from string keys to int values:

var ages map[string]int

The type is written as map[KeyType]ValueType.

Like slices, a map declared with var is nil and you cannot write to it until you initialise it with make:

ages = make(map[string]int)

You can also create and initialise in one step with a map literal:

ages := map[string]int{
    "Alice": 30,
    "Bob":   25,
}

sections.heading: Reading and writing
sections.bodyHtml:
Write a value to a key:

ages["Charlie"] = 35

Read a value back:

fmt.Println(ages["Alice"])  // 30

Delete a key:

delete(ages, "Bob")

The comma ok idiom

Reading a key that doesn't exist returns the zero value. To check whether a key exists, use the two-value form:

value, ok := ages["David"]
if !ok {
    fmt.Println("David not found")
}

ok is true if the key exists, false otherwise.

sections.heading: Your turn
sections.bodyHtml:
Open practice/maps/main.go.

Create a map from student names to grades. Add three students, look up one by name, and print the result with the comma ok idiom.

questions.prompt: How do you declare a map from string keys to int values?

options.label: Removes every entry from the map
Removes the entry with key "key" from map m
Crashes if the key doesn't exist

questions.prompt: If m["missing"] returns 0, how do you tell whether "missing" was actually in the map?

options.label: Check if the result equals nil
Use the two-value form: val, ok := m["missing"]
Call len(m) to see if the map is empty

exercises.title: Using a map
exercises.instructions: Edit main.go to create a map of student grades, add three entries, then look up one by name and print whether it was found.
